title: Tapping for "Is This Too Much?" Pricing Fear
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the wobble that arrives before you name your price
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the pricing freeze that arrives the moment you consider your full number. Taps through the fear of overcharging, the pull to lower before anyone flinches, and the reframe that price comes from value rather than from what feels defensible.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use before writing a quote, setting a rate, or raising prices — any moment when the number in your head shrinks before you have said it.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the pricing wobble: the number that shrinks the moment you imagine saying it. Taps through the fear of being too expensive, the pull to lower before anyone responds, and the shift toward pricing from value rather than from what feels safe to ask.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point on the side of your hand and say each statement once:
Even though I lower the number before anyone has a chance to say no, I accept where I am with this.
Even though I price from the wobble rather than from what the work is worth, there is something more solid underneath.
Even though saying a full price feels like asking for too much, I am open to a different relationship with this.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: Is this too much?
Side of Eye: What if they say it's not worth it?
Under Eye: I drop the price before they even ask.
Under Nose: Underpricing feels safer than rejection.
Chin: The wobble arrives before the quote does.
Collarbone: I price from fear, not from what this is worth.
Under Arm: What if my number is wrong?
Top of Head: The price goes down before the conversation starts.
Reframe
Eyebrow: The number can come from value instead.
Side of Eye: My price is information, not a verdict.
Under Eye: I can name it and let them respond.
Under Nose: My work has worth that exists before the wobble.
Chin: I can stay with the full number.
Collarbone: The price reflects the work, not the anxiety.
Under Arm: I am allowed to charge for what this is worth.
Top of Head: I price clearly, from value, not from fear.
Say the price out loud once, at its full amount, before you quote it to anyone.
Where this comes from
Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).
